responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Epistemological flaws in NICE review methodology and its impact on recommendations for psychodynamic psychotherapies for complex and persistent depression

Susan McPherson , Felicitas Rost , Joel Town and Allan Abbass

Allan Abbass

Halifax
Canada
Faculty of Medicine, Centre for Emotions and Health

London
UK

Halifax
Canada
Faculty of Medicine, Department of Psychiatry

Colchester
UK
School of Health and Social Care

Allan Abbass  

RCTs, evidence based practice, NICE guidelines, psychodynamic psychotherapy, chronic depression, complex depression

The UK draft NICE guideline on depression in adults was sent out for stakeholder consultation between July and September 2017. The final guideline publication date currently remains ‘to be confirmed’. This paper sets out key concerns with the methodology employed in the guideline and its impact on recommendations for psychodynamic psychotherapies for complex and persistent depression. The draft largely ignored the subjective experiences and voices of service users, carers and members of the public, using out of date limited evidence of service user and carer experiences. The guideline fails to incorporate what limited qualitative evidence it reviewed into any treatment recommendations. The Guideline Committee created its own method for categorising depression by longevity, severity and complexity. This has resulted in erroneous and unhelpful classifications of research studies under groupings which do not match clinical and service user experiences or US and European approaches, rendering analyses and conclusions unreliable. We also outline instances of incorrect classification of psychodynamic treatments (such as inclusion of non bona fide treatments or exclusion of relevant bona fide treatment studies) which enables the omission of a recommendation for psychodynamic psychotherapy for complex and persistent depression. Depression is often a long-term condition or can become so if immediate care is inadequate; yet the draft recommendations are all made on the basis of short-term outcome data (with often less than eight weeks between baseline and outcome). NICE guidelines for long-term physical conditions would treat this evidence as inadequate. Finally, the draft guideline used the Grading of Recommendations, Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) system of assessing methodological quality in such a way as to produce a systematic bias in favour of drug trials, selectively omitting trial data with long-term follow-up points and those which used non-symptom outcomes. Herein, we consider the increasingly evident limitations of the paradigm NICE works within for ensuring patient choice and equity of access to a wide range of therapies.

responseSet.title: Guest Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Guest Editorial

Jeremy Clarke

Jeremy Clarke

Centre for Philosophy of Natural and Social Sciences, London School of Economics & Centre for Humanities Engaging Science and Society

Jeremy Clarke   *Email: therapy@practice.demon.co.uk

The day of the bog-standard comprehensive is over (Alistair Campbell, 2001)

This special issue on depression is timed to coincide with a new National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guideline (forthcoming). It will be NICE’s 3rd depression guideline, the first in 2004, the second in 2009. Improving Access to Psychological Therapies (IAPT)’s ubiquitous stepped care model, which has gained international recognition for its ‘world-leading results’ (Stevens, 2017), derives from NICE’s depression guidelines (NICE, 2009). So, it is safe to say this latest iteration will shape the context for NHS-funded talking therapy services for the next decade.

What was the impact of NICE’s first two guidelines? Thanks to two independent National Audits (Royal College of Psychiatrists, 2011, 2013), a wealth of outcomes data from IAPT itself (NHS Digital, 2015–16, 2016–17), the recent Psychiatric Morbidity Survey (McManus, Bebbington, Jenkins, & Brugha, 2016), and detailed prescribing data for anti-depressants (HSCIC, 2016), we are in a good position to track NICE’s impact over the last 10 years.

However, the story can only be understood fully against the context of a radical attempt to create a comprehensive ‘universal access’ talking therapies NHS service, free-at-the-point-of-need to all: ‘You are deprived if you cannot enjoy your life. And the most common cause of deprivation is not poverty or unemployment but mental illness’ (Clark, Fleche, Layard, Powdthavee, & Ward, 2018). Professor Lord Richard Layard’s (2005) flawed but brilliantly original analysis for the Prime Minister’s Strategy Unit argued that depression causes the UK’s single greatest disease burden not only due to its prevalence but also its indirect welfare costs. In other words, invalidity due to chronic depression was to blame for rapidly rising welfare spending occurring across the member countries of the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD), but especially so in the UK (Organisation for Economic Co-operation & Development, 2012). Layard’s solution was simple. NICE had identified cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) as the most cost-effective treatment for common mental illness, Q.E.D. create a comprehensive Improving Access to CBT service, ‘which will pay for itself’. In 2007, Layard and I met with Treasury officials at Number 10 Downing Street.1 We secured a business case for investing in talking therapies to the tune of £300 million in its first three years. IAPT went on to get £400 million under the Coalition; and then a further £600 million from George Osborne2’s spending review.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

London
UK

Jessica Yakeley   jyakeley@tavi-port.nhs.uk

This issue of the journal focuses on the importance of relationships within the psychotherapeutic endeavour – a viewpoint which, by being almost ubiquitously taken for granted within contemporary psychoanalysis, may be overshadowed by more ‘exciting’ developments in psychoanalytic technique and applications. The forthcoming papers bring us back to the fundamental nature of human bonding, how this becomes manifest in the bi-directional field of communications between patient and therapist, and how intrapsychic disturbances can be healed through interpersonal interactions.

In ‘Team splitting and the “borderline personality”: A relational reframe’, Huw Green addresses the wide-spread concept of team splitting. Drawing on both the British and American psychoanalytic literature, Green reviews the historical development, from Tom Main’s (1957) classic paper The Ailment, Isabel Menzies Lyth’s (1960) application of Melanie Klein’s ideas to the training of nurses in a general hospital, and the study by Stanton and Schwartz (1954) of the dynamics of an American psychotherapeutic hospital, to prevailing psychoanalytic explanations which centre around the idea that splits within the team are the result of the defensive processes of projection and projective identification from borderline patients. These formulations, he suggests, have led to simplistic accounts where the patient is blamed, and neglect the contributions from staff, as well as patients, to the team’s pathological dynamics. He critiques this viewpoint from contemporary dialectical, feminist and social-justice perspectives and focuses on understanding team splitting within a psychoanalytic relational framework influenced by object relations theory in which projections are not seen as unidirectional from specific types of disturbed patients ‘manipulating’ or ‘doing’ something to the staff team, but understood as also arising from the predispositions and unconscious conflicts and motivations of team members, both individually and collectively.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

London
UK
jyakeley@tavi-port.nhs.uk

Jessica Yakeley  

Although it may be a truism that the expansion of different types of psychotherapy invented and practiced has become unlimited and unregulated, the wide array of different psychotherapeutic modalities that have emerged from the psychoanalytic and psychodynamic traditions alone contribute to a state of confusion whereby both practitioners and patients struggle to keep up with the development of new therapies and iterations of existing psychotherapy modalities. The situation within the National Health Service in the UK may present a slightly less complex picture in that specific psychological treatments are increasingly likely to only be available if they can demonstrate sufficient empirical evidence for their effectiveness to convince commissioners to pay for them, and therefore the range of therapies on offer is more limited. Nevertheless, the original tripartite division of psychological therapies into cognitive-behavioural, psychodynamic and systemic based on distinct theoretical frameworks, which has guided training and provision for many years, has been replaced, at least for the treatment of some conditions (e.g. personality disorder), by a multitude of newer modalities that combine elements from the original trio as well as other approaches, including the interpersonal and humanistic traditions.

Where does this leave psychoanalytic psychotherapy and its applications in the NHS, which is the specific remit of this journal? Arguably, when the journal was founded 35 years ago, there was less confusion than today in defining the theoretical framework and techniques of psychoanalytic psychotherapy (albeit allowing for differences based on the particular psychoanalytic school, usually within the British psychoanalytic community, informing the practitioner i.e. Kleinian, Independent and Contemporary Freudian traditions). However, in recent years, the descriptor ‘psychoanalytic’ has become increasingly replaced by ‘psychodynamic’, and although some might argue that the terms are synonymous, others would suggest that psychodynamic psychotherapies are based on a broader theoretical framework combining psychoanalytic with attachment theory, and in some cases findings from neuroscience, in which it is proposed that the person’s innate predispositions and their interpersonal, intersubjective and embodied experiences of both their social world and internal worlds build up from childhood to form their representations and object relationships. This widening of the theoretical paradigm has also produced a relational shift in psychotherapeutic technique, with more emphasis on exploring the patient’s relationships, including that with the therapist, in the here and now rather than in the patient’s history; and a focus on the patient’s mental processes and reflective capacity, rather than exploration and interpretation of the patient’s unconscious fantasies. Psychodynamic therapies also tend to be shorter in duration and at least partially manualised to facilitate adherence to the specific model and empirical evaluation.

[[RunningHead]]

‘Post-natal’ depression in fathers, or Early Fatherhood Depression

Sameer Sarkar

Sameer Sarkar

Aldershot
UK
DCMH Aldershot

UK

Sameer Sarkar   *Email: adhd.uk@gmail.com
fatherhood, post-natal depression, masculinity, attachment, gender role

Both men and women are known to suffer from post-natal depression. However, there has been little attention paid to the psychodynamic genesis of post-natal depression in fathers. In this paper, I describe case histories of post-natal depression in two first time fathers and offer a formulation for understanding post-natal depression in such cases. I suggest a range of reasons for the failure to recognize this condition; and possible therapeutic interventions.

[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jessica Yakeley

Jessica Yakeley

London
UK

Jessica Yakeley   *Email: jyakeley@tavi-port.nhs.uk

The four papers in this issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy are quite different in their subject matter, but seem to me to be linked in thinking about the creation of spaces, in different sensory modalities (auditory, visual, verbal and experiential) and in different settings (a child and adolescent mental health service (CAMHS), a community-based day centre, a research interview and a Balint group), where security, growth, exploration of ideas and creativity can be fostered in the minds and interpersonal spheres of the patients, clinicians, students and researchers under study.

The first two papers describe the work of non-verbal therapies – art therapy and music therapy, respectively – at a time when creative therapies are under threat within the increasingly financially constrained NHS, often due to the perception that they are not essential in the treatment of mental health difficulties. It is therefore heartening to publish accounts of on-going work of two of these treatments.

‘Going backstage: a psychoanalytically informed study with amateur musicians in adult mental health services’ by Lorcan Fingleton, John O’Connor and Greg Stynes is a very moving account of a qualitative study exploring the meaning of playing music in six men who attended mental health services and were amateur musicians. This paper addresses a little explored area, namely the therapeutic role of such music playing in the maintenance of mental health. Following a useful review of the literature on the psychological function of music and the growing evidence base for its effectiveness in ameliorating a range of mental disorders, the authors describe the results of the open interviews with the six participants that they conducted, in which they facilitated a free-association style of responses to elicit how their music playing had impacted on their mental health in both positive and negative ways. Fingleton, O’Connor and Stynes applied a psychoanalytically informed qualitative analysis to the transcribed interviews, which revealed four inter-related themes: ‘Left on the outside’; ‘Playing and linking’; ‘A beautiful gift to myself’; and ‘Frustrating play’. Drawing on the ideas of Freud, Winnicott, Fairbairn, Kohut and others, they discuss the role of music as a transitional object, the narcissistic and containing functions of music and its conceptualisation as an exhibitionist act.

[[RunningHead]]

Balint groups in undergraduate medical education: a systematic review

Alice Monk , Daniel Hind and Helen Crimlisk

Helen Crimlisk

Sheffield
UK
The Medical School

Sheffield
UK
Clinical Trials Research Unit, ScHARR

Sheffield
UK
School of Health and Related Research (ScHARR)

Helen Crimlisk  

systematic review, narrative synthesis, medical education, medical students, patient-centred, Balint

Objective: Though Balint groups feature increasingly in UK medical school curricula, there is no evidence-based consensus on what undergraduates might gain from participation, and how this might happen. To address this, we systematically reviewed primary research studies involving medical student Balint groups and their relationship with patient-centredness, using narrative synthesis. Data sources: Four major databases were searched from origin until 9 September 2016. The Journal of the Balint Society was hand-searched from 1971 until 9 September 2016. Study selection: English language studies reporting quantitative and/or qualitative methods examining Balint groups in medical students vs. other/no comparator. Results: Eight studies were included. Quantitative findings report statistically significant improvements to student’s empathy and intellectual interest following group participation vs. control (p = 0.03, p = 0.046, respectively). Discussion content was similar across high-income countries. There was considerable heterogeneity when students rated the efficacy of groups. All evidence had high or unclear risk of bias, or was of medium/low quality. Conclusion: Balint groups might help medical students to become more patient-centred, by increasing students’ empathic abilities and supporting their personal and professional growth. Groups are more subjectively effective when optional rather than compulsory. Discussion content is comparable to groups in continuing medical education.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The new analyst’s guide to the galaxy: questions about contemporary psychoanalysis

Sarah Jones

Sarah Jones

UK

Sarah Jones   *Email: Sarah.Jones@nottshc.nhs.uk

This book is essentially a conversation between Antonino Ferro (a highly experienced psychoanalyst) and Luca Nicoli (a relatively new psychoanalyst). Nicoli is curious about the analytic world that Ferro inhabits. Nicoli describes a passion for psychoanalysis, but is also saddened by his sense that the institution is now perceived by many as old-fashioned and falling behind in the face of newer, more fashionable approaches to therapy.

Nicoli acknowledges his trepidation with regard to moving beyond Freud and more traditional concepts, but equally seems to yearn for a more contemporary and relevant psychoanalysis. Enter Ferro. Nicoli describes him as a psychoanalyst following in the footsteps of Bion and representing a shift in focus from unconscious content (a Freudian concept) to the narrative and metaphorical understanding of the relationship between the analyst and the patient.

To put it simply: the format of the book is that Nicoli asks questions and Ferro answers ….

What ensues is an interesting and at times a whirlwind tour through various aspects of contemporary psychoanalysis through the lens of Ferro’s practice. The journey ranges broadly from very practical considerations such as fees and frequency of sessions, through to the more cerebral notions of reverie and daytime dreaming. There is discussion of how to work with imagery and create ideas for films and characters where the patient plays a leading role, and how to follow the unconscious as it manifests within the analytic space. Ferro also describes a process of ‘co-construction, co-narration’ between patient and analyst to develop mental life.

Ferro asserts his view that there is a risk of getting too weighted down with carrying 100 years of psychoanalytic history around with us. While I feel he respects and values the work of Freud and Klein and feels the classics are a helpful starting point, he urges us to move on and essentially ‘travel light’ so we are open to new exciting ideas and theories. He cites individuals such as Bion, Ogden and Grotstein as examples of free and forward thinkers.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychotherapy for people diagnosed with schizophrenia

Bent Rosenbaum

Bent Rosenbaum

Denmark
Senior psychiatrist, MDSci, Psychiatric Center Copenhagen & Adj professor

Bent Rosenbaum   *Email: d137367@dadlnet.dk

Andrew Lotterman’s book ‘Psychotherapy for people diagnosed with schizophrenia’ is an important book, rich in information and reflections, which presents both clear theoretical arguments and clinical descriptions. It is a revised version of Lotterman’s book from 1996, appearing now at an important moment in the ongoing discussion within psychiatry, psychoanalysis and psychology about how to help people who are diagnosed with the severe disturbances of schizophrenia.

For two to three decades, American psychiatry (Lotterman is an American psychiatrist and psychoanalyst) has had a negative attitude towards psychoanalytic and psychodynamic approaches to help those suffering from schizophrenia spectrum psychosis. For instance, Klerman (1984) stated ‘the evidence from at least half a dozen studies would indicate that no further research on the intensive individual psychotherapy of schizophrenics based on psychodynamic or interpersonal principles is warranted’. But Lotterman points out how different psychiatric papers, even in respected journals, refer to each other in their statements that there are indications that psychodynamic psychotherapy may be potentially toxic (as Scott and Dixon expressed it in Schizophrenia Bulletin, 1995) – all based on very few anecdotal cases!

The toxic or aggressive attitude, if one may describe it in these terms, in the US around the status of psychoanalytic psychotherapy for persons with psychiatric diagnoses, especially the severe psychiatric disorders, is a complex matter. It may have historical reasons based on the dominant status psychoanalysis had in the US psychiatry in the 1950s and 1960s, followed by the final descent for psychoanalysis with the arrival of the symptom-, syndrome- and behaviour-based DSM-III (in 1980) and the subsequent promotion of ‘the decade of the brain’ in the 1990s. The negative attitude towards psychoanalytic psychotherapy may also have politico-economical reasons behind it. The health care system in the US may be more privatised than, for example, Europe (even though European Health Systems are very diversified), and private insurance companies aim at short and cheap solutions for circumscribed mental problems (symptoms, behaviour). Finally, one must not forget Frieda From-Reichmann’s words from her long-term experience treating patients with psychosis: ‘Severe anxiety, withdrawn loneliness, aggressive hatred and lack of improvement of the psychotic patient may be contagious to some psychotherapists’. These counter-transference reactions do not only concern psychotherapists in the narrow sense, but also psychiatrists working in hospitals or private practice. Taking pleasure in the growth of another person and being able to accept the slow pace of development are major difficulties when you work in a health care system in which effectiveness and a quick turn-over of patients are a hallmark and idealised feature.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Unrepressed unconscious, implicit memory, and clinical work

Cathryn Richardson

Cathryn Richardson

UK
Clinical Psychologist

Cathryn Richardson   *Email: Cathryn.Richardson@leicspart.nhs.uk

How do we understand the pre-verbal, pre-symbolic part of the mind which is structurally unconscious? How does it differ from the repressed, dynamic unconscious with which we are so much more familiar? Guiseppe Craparo and Clara Mucci attempt to provide a clearer understanding of the concept of the unrepressed unconscious in this edited book, which brings together contributions from some of the leading figures in this developing field. They are both psychoanalytic psychotherapists and academics whose focus on bringing together conceptual and applied understanding is helpful for the clinician.

In his foreword, Peter Fonagy notes that the book approaches the topic of the unrepressed unconscious from six ‘distinct perspectives that share the wish to integrate contemporary neuroscience with psychoanalytic perspectives’, with each chapter taking a focus on different aspects of the matter at hand, including links with memory, attachment process and trauma. It is indeed the case that the variety of perspectives presented in the book provides a breadth of thinking and evidence to consider, each contributor presenting thoughts on the conjunction between neuroscience and psychoanalysis, as well as the implications for clinical practice.

Mark Solms looks at the way in which modern thinking and research into the nature of the unconscious requires major revisions of Freud’s original theory – perhaps most startlingly the proposition (drawn from the work of Jaak Panksepp and others) that it is consciousness that resides at the deepest level of the mind and more primitive parts of the brain, and that it is the higher brain systems that are unconscious, ‘borrowing’ consciousness through associative links to the lower system. Meanwhile Mauro Mancia presents the role of long-term memory systems and their relation to the unrepressed unconscious, drawing in the contributions of neuroscience and molecular biology and making helpful illustrative links to the psychoanalytic therapy process. Giovanni Liotti’s chapter concentrates on attachment perspectives, taking issue with what he views as Mancia’s ‘restricted’ conceptualisation of the unrepressed unconscious (of an unrepressed unconscious comprised of specific experiences encoded in the earliest stages of life and implicitly remembered) and arguing instead for an ‘extended’ conceptualisation of an unrepressed unconscious as a ‘general modality of pre-verbal and pre-reflective consciousness that operates through the arc of a lifetime’. He uses evolutionary and motivational theory to evidence his argument that attachment theory supports the ‘extended’ concept of the unrepressed unconscious, highlighting the connection between trauma-induced dissociation within the unrepressed unconscious and infant attachment disorganisation.

Maurizio Pompili

Maurizio Pompili

Rome
Italy
Department of Neurosciences, Mental Health and Sensory Organs, Suicide Prevention Center, Sant’Andrea Hospital

Maurizio Pompili   Corresponding author. Email: maurizio.pompili@uniroma1.it
suicide, psychodynamic psychotherapy; narcissism, psychological pain

Suicide is among the leading causes of death worldwide, accounting for almost one million annual deaths globally. The author presents an exploration into the suicidal mind using Freud's cathexis and decathexis theory. This model describes the patient’s withdrawal from reality and the need to gratify libidinal and aggressive drives in the internal world. Theories and beliefs of other authors are used to introduce the importance of early disturbed relationships with parental figures. Working with the particular subtype of suicidal patient described in this essay may be a challenge for clinicians. A psychodynamic approach may help in the understanding of the patient’s complex and private world of psychological pain. If unresolved, the patient’s conflicts may precipitate suicide.

The trouble with illness: how illness and disability affect relationships

Julian Stern

Julian Stern

London
UK
Consultant Psychiatrist in Psychotherapy

Julian Stern   *Email: jstern@tavi-port.nhs.uk

This is an accessible and thoughtful book by Julia Segal, who has not only published work on Melanie Klein, but has also worked both for the NHS and privately as a counsellor for people affected by neurological and other physical health conditions. The book is aimed primarily at those who work, or whose life brings them into contact, with those who are ill or newly disabled in one way or another. As she writes,

Counsellors, psychologists, psychotherapists, professional carers and nurses are the main professional audience but many other healthcare professionals find themselves having to deal with the emotional and relational impact of these conditions on themselves as well as on their clients. And of course, anyone who themselves has a health condition or belongs to a family in which someone else does, or as a child was concerned about a parent’s health recognise their own experience and I hope see it in a new light. (p. 23)

Segal writes that the book is ‘about the effects of living with illness and changing disability on the mind, on thoughts, on feelings, and on the way people live their lives. It is about caring and being cared for, and about not caring and resisting dependency’ (p. 12). Her style is discursive, accessible, and the book is packed with short vignettes, as well as many verbatim quotes from patients, family members or carers. She makes constant reference to her own work, and the book is somewhat biased towards those suffering from neurological conditions such as multiple sclerosis and Parkinson’s disease, presumably a reflection of her own professional experience. Throughout the book one is reminded of her many years of experience, her compassion and the importance of a benign thoughtful other in the lives of patients, their family members and indeed, the healthcare professionals looking after them.

There are some particularly important and moving chapters. Chapter 8 is entitled ‘Damage to Capacities to Think or Feel’, and here she describes the situation in which a family member or partner is losing some of their cognitive capacities, and how difficult it is to discuss matters. Chapter 9 is entitled ‘Parents’, and in this chapter she focuses on the effects on children of parental illness, how parents may be able to help their children, and the impact of one’s own personal history 91

The process of change in ethnic minority males undergoing psychodynamic psychotherapy: a detailed comparison of two cases

Tohar Dolev , Sigal Zilcha-Mano , Harold Chui , Marna Barrett , Kevin McCarthy and Jacques Barber

Jacques Barber

Philadelphia
PA
USA

Haifa

Department of Psychology

Jacques Barber  

alliance, therapeutic process, case studies, ethnic minority groups, psychodynamic treatment, rupture resolution processes

Better understanding of the connection between therapeutic processes and outcomes in minority groups can help design and use culturally adapted treatments. To explore the active ingredient in the therapeutic process, the present case study compared two ethnic minority male clients, recruited as part of a randomized controlled trial, one with a good outcome, the other with a poor one. The 12-item Working Alliance Inventory-Observer (S-WAI-O) coding system was used to capture the process of change, alongside a qualitative analysis of content. The cases were identified based on their change in pre- to post-treatment scores on the Beck Depression Inventory and the Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression. The findings suggest a rupture-resolution process in the good outcome case, including a process of negotiation of the alliance and work on issues of trust. In contrast, the poor outcome case showed strong and steady alliance, but context analysis pointed to withdrawal ruptures. Although it is difficult to generalize from a two-case study analysis, the present work suggests that building and negotiating alliance with minority clients has a potential for treatment success.

responseSet.title: The theoretical basis of the Conversational Model of Therapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The theoretical basis of the Conversational Model of Therapy

Else Guthrie and Jim Moorey

Jim Moorey

Ambleside
Cumbria
UK

Psychological Medicine

Jim Moorey  

Conversational Model, Psychodynamic Interpersonal Therapy, theory, forms of feeling, hypotheses

The Conversational Model of Therapy, which is also known as Psychodynamic-Interpersonal Therapy is an evidence-based psychological treatment. The model has a set of distinctive therapist behaviours, which can be taught and learnt in a short space of time. Although the model can be practised without an appreciation of the theory which underpins it, the theoretical basis of the model is rooted in psychodynamic ideas, and it shares many common theoretical approaches with other psychodynamic models. In this article, we describe the theoretical underpinnings of the model, include a description of basic model behaviours, an explanation of why these behaviours are important, and how they directly relate to and are derived from the theoretical stance. We also provide a case example, and show why the model can be practised without detailed theoretical knowledge to good effect, if its common guiding principles are followed.

Receiving slaps, receiving words. The violence of separation in a public service time limited psychotherapy group for adolescents

Laura Balottin , Valentina Palvarini and Maurizio Salis

Maurizio Salis

Padua
Italy

Padua

Maurizio Salis  

time-limited group psychoanalytic psychotherapy, institutional group for adolescents, verbal and physical violence, trauma, countertransference

The authors propose a reflection on a time-limited psychoanalytic group for children, adolescents and families, run in a public service. Some patients in the group give accounts of violent relationships and traumatic experiences. The accounts of violent, interpersonal and intrapsychic relationships influence the group’s capacity of thinking, which leads to difficulties in the processing of symbolic aspects. The fundamental question is that of whether it is possible to work in a time-limited, institutional, psychoanalytical framework, in which psychotherapists have to attempt to give meaning to experiences which take place in a repetitive present, in a space/time in which the prospect of a conclusion appears to be inconceivable. By means of a clinical example, and with particular emphasis on the first sessions and the dream brought up by a patient who has been a victim of domestic violence, the authors discuss the possibility of modifying, chiefly from within, the feeling of the immutability of early psychic material, which is itself intrinsically violent.

[[RunningHead]]

Catch 22: the inflammatory nature of insight

Marcus Evans

Marcus Evans

Marcus Evans   *Email: marcusevans@hotmail.co.uk
insight, psychotic, inflammatory, psychic retreat, defence

I will describe a man I saw in psychoanalytic psychotherapy with a history of serious drug abuse and violent psychotic episodes. At the start of therapy my patient had little capacity to tolerate the anxiety, depression or the inevitable frustrations involved in facing reality. Over time and with the support of the therapy he was able to increase his capacity for self observation noticing the way he withdrew from the world of shared reality into psychotic states of mind in order to avoid painful feelings of rage, humiliation and shame. In the book ‘Catch 22ʹ, Joseph Heller described the paradox of pilots who wish to claim insanity in order to avoid fatal flying missions, while the authorities maintain that in claiming insanity to avoid suicidal missions the individual demonstrates a sane awareness of the insanity of the missions! This insight is deemed to be evidence of the individual’s sanity and consequently their plea of insanity is denied. In this paper, I will show how Mr. A’s developing capacity for reflective functioning lead to a ‘Catch 22’ as the insight he needed as part of the process of recovery threatened to overwhelm him with depressing realities about the extent of his illness.

[[RunningHead]]

The secret sorrows of men: impact of Dynamic Interpersonal Therapy on ‘masculine depression’

Joanna Dognin and Cory Chen

Cory Chen

New York
NY
USA
Department of Psychiatry

New York
NY
USA
Department of Veterans Affairs

Cory Chen  

Depression, Dynamic Interpersonal Therapy, masculinities, veteran mental health, psychotherapy

Men are underrepresented among depression diagnoses, yet are more likely to engage in self-harm. Masculinities research has sought to better understand the mechanisms through which men may conceal or suppress depression, and has suggested that traditional diagnostic criteria may fail to recognize the many ways that gender may influence the presentation of negative affect. Ideals of hegemonic masculinity dominate military culture, making mental health treatment in a veterans’ hospital an ideal setting to work through the influence of rigid gender norms on interpersonal relationships. Relational therapy can be particularly useful to help some men reconstruct their sense of self in relation to their own rigid gender norms. Dynamic interpersonal therapy is a powerful therapeutic modality that can help men recognize and connect with parts of themselves that have been previously disowned. By giving voice to these more vulnerable affects, men may be freed up to connect to and communicate their own longing for care in relationships. We will present three case vignettes to illustrate this approach among American male veterans seeking psychotherapy at a Veterans Administration (VA) Hospital in New York City.

responseSet.title: The interpersonal structure of depression
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The interpersonal structure of depression

Matthew Ratcliffe

Matthew Ratcliffe

Vienna
Austria
Department of Philosophy

Matthew Ratcliffe   *Email: matthew.ratcliffe@univie.ac.at
depression, existential feeling, interpersonal connection, isolation, phenomenology, possibility, self-regulation

This paper addresses the manner in which alterations of interpersonal experience are (a) central to most of those predicaments labelled as ‘depression’, and (b) inextricable from other, seemingly distinct depression-symptoms. I sketch an approach inspired by the phenomenological tradition of philosophy, which emphasizes how depression-experiences involve profound changes in one’s sense of possibility. In so doing, I show how anticipated and actual interactions with other people shape and re-shape experience of the wider world by imbuing it with certain distinctive types of possibility. It follows from this that a shift in how one anticipates, experiences, and relates to other people in general also amounts to a shift in the types of possibility offered by the world. My discussion is concerned primarily with the structure of depression-experiences, rather than with causes or treatment. However, I conclude by tentatively addressing some implications for the latter.

I expect NHS bodies to act without delay … the prime minister and I have made mental health services a personal priority: from record spending, to our zero suicide ambition, to the new law on parity of esteem, we are seeing progress, but there is still more to do: any avoidable harm is simply unacceptable.

– Health and Social Care Secretary Jeremy Hunt quoted in ‘Hundreds of mental health patients died after NHS care failures’, Duncan, Campbell, & Bawden, 2018

The market is always in danger of disintegrating into a fragmented world in which there is an attempt to deny the interdependence between individuals, disciplines and clinical areas. Manic defences are employed in order to triumph over feelings of vulnerability, inadequacy and failure. These states of mind are then projected around the social system. The world starts to be divided between survivors and casualties, winners and losers in a paranoid schizoid position. This superior and defensive state of mind undermines the attitude required for reverie and learning through experience.

– M. Evans, 2014, p. 200

My first significant contact with someone in a suicidal state of mind took place when I was 21. I was working on the night shift at a hostel for homeless women when a resident talked to me for several hours about her disappointment with professionals and with her family, and her intention to kill herself. I have a vivid memory of travelling home on the bus the next morning through the incongruous, daffodilly spring sunshine, and feeling filled with confusion, concern for her and anxiety about my responsibility for acting on this terrible knowledge. I also remember being shamefully aware of feeling that something horrible had been done to me, or put into me; that I was a victim of something, when (as I saw it at the time) all of my concern and righteous, reparative wishes ought to be on the behalf of the resident. [[RunningHead]]

Beyond Doer and Done to: Recognition Theory, Intersubjectivity and the Third

Haroula Konstantinidou

Haroula Konstantinidou

Leicester
UK
Leicestershire Partnership Trust, Dynamic Psychotherapy Service, Gwendolen House

Haroula Konstantinidou   haroula.konstantinidou@leicspart.nhs.uk

I looked forward to reading Benjamin’s new book Beyond Doer and Done To. My prior experience of the relational school of psychoanalysis was a refreshing alternative to classical analytic thinking. In my preliminary readings, I had struggled to grasp the concept of the Intersubjective Third, particularly its application to clinical practice, and I had been seeking to deepen my understanding of the basic theory and practice of relational psychoanalysis. My expectations were met as this book summarises Jessica Benjamin’s theory of intersubjectivity and further clarifies the concept of the Third. In addition it offers plenty of clinical examples and insights into the actual practice and technique of relational psychoanalysis.

Benjamin revisits her theoretical concepts in every chapter of the book, a repetition which can be tiring at times but allows each chapter to be read as a discreet entity. The first chapter, ‘Beyond Doer and Done to: An Intersubjective View of Thirdness’, provides a useful theoretical introduction to Recognition Theory, Intersubjectivity and the concept of the Third. Benjamin explains how the relational approach departs from the mainstream analytic paradigm; for example, in our formulation of the patient’s past and present, we often conceptualise relationships that are complementary and reactive with roles such as abuser and abused, controlling and controlled, and doer and done to. Benjamin shows how this can perpetuate frustrating early experiences where only one truth – often the analyst’s truth – can survive and the other needs to be destroyed. In her words: ‘Either I am crazy or you are’ (p. 25). She invites us to rethink our analytic stance and to co-create an experience with the patient that goes beyond the above complementary dynamic. This joint venture introduces us to thirdness, a process which is shared and constantly co- created between the analyst and patient and is liable to continuous ruptures and repairs. Starting from a developmental perspective, and incorporating ideas from object relations and attachment-based theories, Benjamin discusses the different aspects of the Third, describing what she calls the Rhythmic and Differentiating Thirds. The Rhythmic Third refers to the affective attunement, accommodation and co-regulation that takes place in the mother– infant/analyst–patient dyad. The Differentiating Third enables the existence of two separate minds and refers to the mother’s/analyst’s ability to represent their own distress as distinct from the child’s/patient’s. responseSet.authorCitation: Leonidaki, V., Lemma, A. & Hobbis, I.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The active ingredients of dynamic interpersonal therapy (DIT): an exploration of clients’ experiences

Venetia Leonidaki , Alessandra Lemma and Imogen Hobbis

Imogen Hobbis

London
UK

Norwich
UK
Deparment of Clinical Psychology

Colchester
UK
The School of Health and Human Sciences

Imogen Hobbis  

dynamic interpersonal therapy (DIT), brief psychodynamic psychotherapy, interpetative phenomenological analysis (IPA), qualitative research, clients’ experiences

The current article focuses on client’s perspectives of the mechanisms of change in Dynamic Interpersonal Therapy (DIT), a brief psychodynamic therapy developed for the treatment of depression. Five participants were interviewed and the transcripts were analysed using Interpretative Phenomenological Analysis (IPA). The results point out four subthemes, capturing different active ingredients of DIT: therapy shedding light on previously sealed aspects of self; the relational exchange with the therapist challenging intimate fears; moving towards an interpersonal understanding of their difficulties; and putting the pieces of their lives together into a coherent narrative. Overall participants described an interplay between relational and insight-oriented mechanisms taking place. As part of the former mechanisms, participants reported the development of a secure attachment to their therapist and the provision of a new relational experience, partly via mechanisms corresponding to the concept of transference. Participants reported an increase in insight to have been achieved via an understanding of an interpersonal and emotional pattern as well as via the restoration of a coherent life-story. Processes that theoretically map onto the concept of mentalisation were also reported. The findings are discussed in relation to the DIT and psychodynamic theory about change and to relevant research literature.

responseSet.authorCitation: Fingleton, L., O’Connor, J. & Stynes, G.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Going backstage: a psychoanalytically informed study with amateur musicians in adult mental health services

Lorcan Fingleton , John O’Connor and Greg Stynes

Greg Stynes

Dublin
Ireland
EVE

Dublin
Ireland
School of Psychology

Greg Stynes  

psychoanalytic research interview, amateur musicians, music, qualitative, artist

Many mental health service users engage in potentially therapeutic amateur music practice, though the role of this in the maintenance of mental health is largely unexplored. The application of psychoanalytic ideas to music-playing gives suggestions for how this activity could helpfully sublimate distress. For this study, six men who attend adult mental health services and play a musical instrument took part in three unstructured hour-long interviews. The material was analysed with a psychoanalytically informed method. Four inter-related themes emerged: ‘Left on the outside’; ‘Playing and linking’; ‘A beautiful gift to myself’; and ‘Frustrating play’. In the lives of these amateur musicians, music’s role as a transitional object and means of containing distress is explored. There is discussion of the narcissistic function of music and the dynamics around music as an exhibitionistic act. The value of paying attention to music in the life of a patient is considered.

[[RunningHead]]

Mobilizing self through short-term dynamic interpersonal psychotherapy: a preliminary report

Janine Stevenson , Joan Haliburn , Shaun Halovic , Anthony Korner and Loyola McLean

Loyola McLean

Wentworthville
Australia
Psychological Medicine

Wentworthville
Australia
Westmead Psychotherapy Program for complex traumatic disorders - Psychological Medicine

Wentworthville
Australia
Psychological medicine

Loyola McLean  

short-term, time-limited, psychotherapy, research, trauma

Background: The key features of short-term dynamic interpersonal psychotherapy (STDIP) are outlined along with the background of its development. Preliminary data demonstrating functional improvement in a small case series of patients is presented.

Methods: Nineteen patients were evaluated at commencement and end of therapy in a pilot study. Instruments used were the Childhood Trauma Questionnaire (CTQ); Beck Depression Inventory (BDI); Brief Symptom Inventory (BSI); COPE (Coping Style Questionnaire); Relationship Styles Questionnaire (RSQ) and Social Adjustment Scale-Self Report (SAS-SR).

Results: This cohort had significantly elevated scores on the CTQ. They demonstrated substantial improvement on measures including the BDI, and several subscales of BSI, COPE, RSQ and SAS-SR.

Conclusions: The small numbers and naturalistic design preclude firm conclusions however the results suggest that STDIP can be used to successfully treat patients, notwithstanding a history of childhood trauma.

responseSet.title: For where you are gathered, I will be: experiencing the third
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

For where you are gathered, I will be: experiencing the third

Claire Bacha

Claire Bacha

Prestwich, Manchester

Claire Bacha   *Email: claire1bacha@gmail.com

What is it like to do psychoanalytic psychotherapy in a group? How does it work when the analytic couple becomes a three or more? What happens to the analytic conversation? To transference? What happens to intimacy and belonging? What happens to the individual in the group and to the group in the individual? What do these ideas have to do with working in psychoanalytic psychotherapy in general? Are these ideas relevant to the living of everyday life and the promotion of living and seeking, more than just surviving?

Nick Barwick and Martin Weegman have written a rich and readable contribution to the theory and practice of analytic psychotherapy in groups, and also outside of groups.

There are many aspects to this book. For one, it is an impressive work of scholarship and theoretical integration. In his prologue, Nick Barwick presents his intention of writing a ‘… concise introduction to the field of group analysis, organised and referenced in such a way that readers looking for a fulsome one-stop meal will find sufficient fare, while others, wishing to explore further will find ample signposts of where to go’ (p. xix). Barwick and Weegman have certainly accomplished this intention.

Group analysis is a form of psychoanalytic psychotherapy and is capable of informing individual professional work in the general field of mental health, as well as offering an alternative or addition to individual work for many patients and clients. Group analysis is particularly good at unpicking and integrating difference and blocks to communication that might otherwise become polarised. Barwick and Weegman illustrate this characteristic of group analysis by figuratively putting a multiplicity of psychoanalysts, group analysts and others into a very large and diverse imagined group and facilitating a conversation between them. So, although presenting their book as a review of the group analytic literature, Barwick and Weegman also do an important job of pulling together and integrating ideas and concepts which are often presented as splits. The very feel of the fabric of the text carries a sense of being in a group and witnessing the flow of ideas exchanged and digested.

responseSet.title: Screen relations: the limits of computer-mediated psychoanalysis and psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Screen relations: the limits of computer-mediated psychoanalysis and psychotherapy

Malika Verma

Malika Verma

Kerala
India

Malika Verma   *Email: malika@taraclinic.in @tara_ajay

Having spent 12 years in the UK, working and training as a clincal psychologist and psychoanalytic psychotherapist in the NHS, the idea of running a psychoanalytic practice in a small southern town of India could have seemed like professional hara-kiri...but for Skype. I began practising in India three years ago and moved, as if seamlessly, to doing therapy over Skype. I had some inhibitions to begin with. Would the screen block the patient’s projections? Would it interfere with my countertransference? But such concerns soon receded into the background. I took on Skype patients just as I would local ones who came to my clinic. In my mind, there was no distinction. Until eventually, I started feeling that something was amiss, but I seemed to lack the words and the experience to understand or explain to myself what that was.

Hence, I am thankful to Gillian Russell for writing a very readable, well researched and well thought-out book. Reading it felt like I was having a personal conversation with the author, and as soon as a question, concern or argument rose in my mind, there she was attending to precisely that! Eventually I had a framework to understand my experience, and more importantly the experience of my online patients. The book helped me to have discussions with my patients which were not just about paying lip service to the idea of understanding their experience. It was as if for the first time we were able to acknowledge a truth: the truth of our deprivation and our struggle to stay connected.

I feel I need to explain my perspective. I run a small psychoanalytic clinic in a small city in Kerala, India. We have four trainees. They all have their personal therapy online because there are no other qualified psychotherapists in the city. Our city is 10 hours by car from Bangalore, which is home to the most established clinical psychology training in the country. Clinical psychologists are slowly becoming open to the idea of being in personal therapy; however, since all the psychologists have graduated from the same institute and know each other, they have to search for a therapist outside of their city. That brings them to me, the only psychoanalytic psychotherapist practising in the southern half of India. All the psychologists who contacted me were familiar with screen therapy. Although I had no experience of seeing people online, they did. I should perhaps say here that I do, very much to my surprise, have a good local practice with a few patients in analysis.

[[RunningHead]]

A process study of long-term treatment: comparing a successful and a less successful outcome

M. Drapeau , K. Stelmaszczyk , D. Baucom , M. Henry and C. Hébert

C. Hébert

Montreal
Canada
Department of Oncology

Montreal
Canada
Department of Psychiatry

Chapel Hill

USA
Department of Psychology

Montreal
Canada
Department of Educational & Counselling Psychology

C. Hébert  

defense mechanisms, experiencing scale, psychoanalysis, psychodynamic, therapist technique

This study examined therapist technique and patient change in emotional experiencing and defense mechanisms in the successful and unsuccessful long-term psychoanalytic treatments of two male patients. Two consecutive sessions every 6 months were analyzed for each patient. Therapist interventions, patient defense mechanisms, and patient emotional experiencing were assessed using the Psychodynamic Intervention Rating Scale, Defense Mechanism Rating Scale and the Experiencing Scale, respectively. Between and within-session analyses were conducted to determine the impact of the patient’s defensive functioning and experiencing on therapist interventions, and the effect of therapist interventions on those same two patient variables. Pearson’s Coefficient was utilized for between-session analysis; within-session analysis tracking moment-to-moment changes in patient and therapist functioning was performed using lag sequential analysis. Across therapy, therapist use of Interpretive Interventions was associated with increased emotional engagement and decreased defense maturity; the use of Supportive Interventions had the opposite effect. Within-session analysis revealed that use of Supportive Interventions when emotional engagement is low, followed by interpretative interventions, occurred in the successful case. Persistent use of Supportive Interventions in the context of low emotional engagement was observed in the unsuccessful case. This study suggests that failure to stimulate emotional engagement through interpretation can negatively affect therapeutic outcome.

[[RunningHead]]

Influence of childhood trauma on depression in the INDDEP study

Anna Docter , Almut Zeeck , Jörn von Wietersheim and Heinz Weiss

Heinz Weiss

Stuttgart
Germany
Department for Psychosomatic Medicine and Psychotherapy

Frankfurt am Main
Germany

Freiburg
Germany
Department for Psychosomatic Medicine and Psychotherapy

Ulm
Germany
Department for Psychosomatic Medicine and Psychotherapy

Frankfurt am Main
Germany
Department for Psychiatry, Psychosomatic Medicine and Psychotherapy

Heinz Weiss  

childhood trauma, depression, psychosomatic therapy, clinical sample, psychotherapy

Traumatic experiences during childhood have been shown to be associated with a higher risk for depressive disorders, more severe symptoms, higher rates of comorbidity, and poorer response to therapy. This study aims to further investigate these links in a clinical sample, focusing especially on therapeutic outcome. Data were collected in a large naturalistic study on inpatient and day hospital treatment of depression at the beginning, the end and after treatment, with this article being a secondary data analysis. Occurrence of childhood trauma, depressive and other symptoms and therapeutic results were compared using robust MANOVA, multiple regression, mixed ANOVA, and Spearman’s correlation. Results provide support for a relationship between childhood trauma and depressive and general symptom severity, the age of onset of depression and the occurrence of personality disorders and somatic diagnoses. There was a significant reduction of depressive symptomatology after therapy. Patients with childhood trauma did not differ significantly from controls in their response to treatment. Clinicians should take the high probability of childhood trauma in depressive patients and the consequent risk of more severe symptoms into account when taking a history and consider adjusting treatment, even if the present data showed that patients generally benefitted from therapy.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Falling outside of the ‘nice little binary box’: a psychoanalytic exploration of the non-binary gender identity

Mairéad Losty and John O’Connor

John O’Connor

Dublin
Ireland
Department of Psychology, Trinity College, Dublin

John O’Connor  

psychoanalytic interview, qualitative, non-binary gender, non-conformity, identity

The non-binary gender identity has recently emerged on the landscape of gender variance. It captures individuals who may experience a gender identity that is neither exclusively male nor female, is a combination of male and female or is between or beyond genders. The availability of literature exploring the non-binary gender experience is somewhat limited. Consequently, our understanding of the development of ‘self’ amongst such individuals seems to lag behind that of other gender variant identity, in particular the binary transgender identity. A psychoanalytically informed qualitative research design was used to explore the psychological realities of 6 individuals, aged between 19 and 29 years, who identified as gender fluid, genderqueer, polygender, non-binary Trans person, non-binary trans guy and non-binary gender. Analysis focused upon the integration of conscious and unconscious material in order to provide some insight into the participants’ internal world and object-relating. Three core themes emerged from the data: ‘A developing gender identity’, ‘Correct and incorrect language’ and ‘Being seen and unseen’. These themes, as well as the heterogeneity and complexity of non-binary gender identity, are explored within the discussion, with some implications around our clinical and wider cultural response to people who identify themselves in this way.

The Vasarhelyi method of child art psychotherapy: an adjunctive treatment in childhood depression

Aynur Coşkunlu , Ezgi Tanıl , Anne Coffey , Dicle Büyüktaşkın and Aisling Mulligan

Aisling Mulligan

Ankara
Turkey

Dublin

Aisling Mulligan   *Corresponding author. Email: aisling.mulligan@ucd.ie

We describe the use of child art psychotherapy (CAP) following the Vasarhelyi method in an 11-year-old female with a history of anxiety and depression which limited her capacity to attend school and in a 15-year-old male who had a history of depressive symptoms with suicidal thinking. In each case, the use of other therapies such as cognitive behaviour therapy was not possible or unsuccessful. Through the creation of images, each young person revealed concerns which had not been previously verbalised, and recovery was linked in time to the revelation of these worries. We propose the use of the Vasarhelyi method of CAP as an adjunctive treatment of depression in young people.

Melanie Klein Revisited, by Susan Sherwin-White, published posthumously, and Reading Klein, by Margaret and Michael Rustin are both important additions to writings about Klein. In my view, both are excellent.

Sherwin-White has brought together her earlier career as ancient historian and epigrapher with her later equally distinguished career as a child and adolescent psychotherapist. Like the birds described by Iain McGilchrist in The Master and His Emissary (2010), she unites into a coherent and accessible whole the closest right-eyed/left hemisphere attention to detail with a left-eyed/right hemisphere wider world view.

Her focus is on Klein’s psychoanalysis of very young children, from 2 to 6 years, mainly from before she left Berlin in 1926, some written up in The Psycho-Analysis of Children (1932). Sherwin-White’s passion for an accurate understanding of Klein and her work emerges by producing the evidence from both published and unpublished material, the latter from her researches in the Melanie Klein archive in the Wellcome Institute for the History and Understanding of Medicine in London. She also makes full use of recently published translations of Klein’s own clinical notes by Frank (2009) to refute contemporary claims of Klein’s harsh and negative analytic approach, and to correct Alix Strachey’s mistranslations into English which ‘eliminated’ the concept of reparation from her earlier writings.

She has used Klein’s notes and her own researches to extend understanding of Klein’s actual work beyond what can be learnt from the selective clinical material she used to illustrate themes in her published writings. It is helpful to the twenty-first-century child psychotherapist to know that Klein’s young patients threw things at her, attacked, spat and defecated in sessions. [[RunningHead]]

I, Me, You & Us: can a psychodynamic model provide a relational perspective on psychosis?

Justin Stephenson

Justin Stephenson

Exeter
UK
Clinical Education Development and Research (CEDAR)

Justin Stephenson   *Corresponding author. Email:justin@driversa.co.za
psychosis, psychodynamic theory, relational perspective, attachment, object relations

The concept of psychosis existed long before the current diagnostic definitions we have today. However, current opinion and treatment of psychosis is often dominated by a narrow psychiatric discourse. As a result of this conceptualisation, a diagnosis of psychosis can often feel limiting and disempowering for the individual, especially if he or she holds an alternative perspective. A psychodynamic model may therefore provide a method to examine psychosis from a relational perspective; this may, in turn, broaden one’s understanding and experience of a psychotic episode. In this article, two case examples from different cultural perspectives are used to highlight and explore the potential benefit of psychodynamic theory in the formulation and treatment of psychosis. A psychodynamic model using object relations theory is used for the exploration of both internal and external relational experiences. Both psychodynamic and psychiatric rationales are thus challenged to reflect on the impact of rigid perspectives and how they may limit treatment efficacy. Current research and literature around the use of psychodynamic theory in the treatment of psychosis is reviewed in light of this perspective.

[[RunningHead]]

Team splitting and the ‘borderline personality’: a relational reframe

Huw Green

Huw Green

New York
USA
Department of Psychiatry

Huw Green   *Corresponding author. Email: huw.green@gmail.com
splitting, personality disorder, relational theory, borderline

The discussion of splitting within mental health treatment teams emerged from British object relations theory, a movement concerned with understanding the interplay between individuals in terms of their inner lives. Over time, the terminology used in this domain has become reified and splitting has been discussed in terms that are apt to frame the process as patient-precipitated. This is apt to encourage simplistic inferences, and attribute to patients responsibility for events that are beyond their control. In this article, the historical development of the psychoanalytic account of team splitting is examined and contemporary critiques are reviewed. It is suggested that the resultant one-person picture overlooks the ways that psychoanalytic theory has been subject to controversy and revision. In the last section of the article, I propose that relational innovations in psychoanalytic theory provide a way to rediscover the richness of early descriptions of splitting.

[[RunningHead]]

On being surrounded by loss

Paul Hoggett

Paul Hoggett

Bristol
UK
Emeritus of Social Policy

Paul Hoggett   *Email: paul.hoggett@uwe.ac.uk

Is it legitimate and helpful to deploy concepts such as paranoia, hysteria or melancholia outside of the consulting room to understand social, cultural or political phenomena? Renee Lertzman makes a convincing case for just such an approach using the concept of melancholia in order to understand various forms of environmental inaction. She insists that it is too easy to dismiss such inaction as ‘apathy’ but invites us to consider the complex psycho-social processes at work when we face the damage and loss humankind inflicts upon ‘mother earth’.

Lertzman has been involved in environmental communications for many years and her frustrations with the rationalist approaches that dominated this field led her to undertake her own doctorate. Her study is based upon research in the heavily polluted Fox River and Green Bay area of Wisconsin where she carried out fieldwork and in-depth qualitative interviews with a sample of adults, many of whom had lived in the area before it became industrialised and environmentally damaged. What is fascinating is that Lertzman explicitly uses a psychoanalytic lens to understand her interviewee responses. She also used therapeutically informed methods of undertaking interview research. These methods (Clarke & Hoggett, 2009; Hollway & Jefferson, 2012) involved open-ended interviews, alive to the complex defences and positionings that occur in the interview space. Rather than a single interview Lertzman often returned on several occasions to continue the dialogue with her respondents over time.

Her starting point is Freud’s classic exploration in Mourning and Melancholia where he distinguishes between the work of mourning and working through and, on the other hand, that ‘crushed state of melancholia’ (Freud, 1917, p. 248) which is sometimes complaining and clamorous and sometimes quiet and pining. Remember Freud himself has the social dimension of this phenomenon in mind when, right at the start of his essay he notes that whilst the loss may refer to a loved one it may equally refer to what he calls an abstraction ‘such as one’s country, liberty, an ideal and so on’ (p. 243).

Swapna Kongara   *Email: Swapna.kongara@lancashirecare.nhs.uk

This book is a part of the series published for the International Society for Psychological and Social approaches to Psychosis (ISPS), an organization with the core aim of promoting psychological and social approaches to understanding and treating psychosis. I started the book with enthusiasm due to my proclivity towards the ISPS and their work, but also with slight uncertainty and even skepticism due to my lack of any prior knowledge regarding art therapy. I was pleasantly surprised with how gripping I found some of the chapters, and some were particularly touching and inspiring. I was equally frustrated by the complexity of some chapters, especially those that did not have the back up of case material. However, taking time and revisiting difficult concepts has I think greatly increased my understanding of some theoretical aspects related to art therapy in psychosis.

This book is a compilation of chapters by various authors, and presents theoretical and clinical approaches to art therapy in psychosis. It incorporates perspectives not only from different schools of psychoanalysis, but also from several other disciplines including philosophy, ontology, phenomenology, and neurophysiology. I had not been previously aware that psychoanalysis is just one among many elements informing art therapy.

The book describes various methods and techniques of art therapy, including artwork-focused art therapy, the side-by-side approach, dialogical art therapy, and the use of comic strips. These are described using case narratives, and both individual and group therapies are included. I felt touched and inspired by the case narratives that illustrate the role of art therapy in helping patients recover their ability for communication and engagement with others, which is usually profoundly compromised by intolerable psychotic anxieties.

The introductory chapter is I think aptly titled ‘places for mind to heal,’ taken from the words of gratitude of a patient describing the meaning and relevance of places like the art room for people with psychosis. The book powerfully conveys the capacity for art therapy to minimize the requirement for forms of interpersonal relating that are to a greater or lesser extent impossible in psychotic states.
